exemplar.title: M25 #1: Do historians and human scientists have an ethical obligation to follow the directive: "do not ignore contradictory evidence"? Discuss with reference to history and the human sciences.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #1: Do historians and human scientists have an ethical obligation to follow the directive: "do not ignore contradictory evidence"? Discuss with reference to history and the human sciences.

strengths: Clear and sustained focus on the prescribed title.
Effective, concrete examples from both history (Holocaust denial, Tiananmen Square, Lipstadt vs. Irving) and the human sciences (Purdue Pharma, MeadâFreeman debate).
Coherent structure with logically sequenced arguments.
Awareness of different points of view and counterclaims, showing ethical as well as epistemic considerations.
weaknesses: Analysis of how methods and frameworks in each area of knowledge shape the handling of contradictory evidence remains under-developed.
Philosophical references (Kant, Arendt) are invoked descriptively without fully integrating them into a deeper Theory of Knowledge analysis.
Evaluation of counterclaims is brief, and key implications for knowledge production (paradigm shifts, trust in public knowledge) are only touched on.
Citation and sourcing need more critical engagement to assess authority and reliability in a Theory of Knowledge context.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The examinerâs overall mark of 6 places this essay squarely at the lower end of band 5â6 for the âUnderstanding knowledge questionsâ criterion. The work demonstrates a sustained focus on the title, clear and coherent arguments supported by specific, well-chosen examples, and some evaluation of differing viewpointsâqualities that meet the descriptors for this band. However, the analysis often remains descriptive, and the deeper conceptual and methodological evaluation required to move into the mid or upper range of the band is only partially present. Given these strengths and limitations, awarding the full 6 marks ensures the final score accurately reflects the essayâs performance at the threshold of bands 5â6.

annotations.comment: Referencing Google Books lacks full publication details; the student should ensure citations adhere to academic standards and reflect each sourceâs credibility.
The citation list is comprehensive but would benefit from source evaluation within the text to discuss authority and reliability of online versus peer-reviewed materials.
While including primary research by Van Zee is positive, the student should critically assess the studyâs design and limitations when discussing its methodological reliability.
Citing a journalistic source adds perspective, but ToK analysis requires distinguishing between journalistic narratives and scholarly historiography to clarify epistemic status.
The Arendt quotation is apt, but the student should explicate how âtruthâ functions differently within historical and human sciences contexts to deepen conceptual clarity.
The Kantian ethical framework is introduced; however, the link between Kantâs universalizability and knowledge-production practices in the areas of knowledge remains underdeveloped.

The cheating example illustrates the categorical imperative but remains descriptive; the student should analyse how analogous reasoning applies when historians select and evaluate conflicting sources.
The counterclaim that ignoring evidence may foster further discovery is interesting but lacks engagement with ToK concepts such as paradigm shifts or interpretative frameworks.
In the Mead-Freeman debate, interpretative diversity is highlighted; the student should analyse how each anthropologistâs data-collection methods influence the reliability of their evidence.
The introduction restates the title effectively but would benefit from explicitly introducing the central knowledge questions to guide the analysis and sharpen the essayâs focus from the outset.
The thesis statement outlines the argument broadly but would gain precision by specifying the key knowledge questions that will structure the subsequent analysis.
The transition into the Purdue case is abrupt; the student should use clearer signposting to maintain logical flow and guide the reader through the essayâs structure.
The Purdue Pharma example is a powerful, concrete illustration of ethical failure; real-world evidence strengthens the discussionâs relevance to human sciences.
Quantifying the human cost underlines the stakes, but the essay would gain depth by reflecting on how human sciences methodologies influence the acceptance and evaluation of contradictory data.
This discussion of consequences is clear, but the student should explore the epistemological implications for public trust as a key Theory of Knowledge concept.
The direct contrast between Mead and Freeman is described, but the student could probe how their respective theoretical frameworks shape what counts as valid evidence.
The final summary restates key points yet does not introduce novel insights; consider reflecting on shared epistemic norms and the role of knowledge communities in upholding ethical standards.

Linking cultural determinism and biologically informed perspectives demonstrates insight into methodological biases; this could be extended with evaluation of ethical implications for evidence acknowledgment.
Applying Kantâs ethics to human sciences is promising; the student should clarify how moral duties intersect with epistemic responsibilities in the production of knowledge.
The assertion that historians must acknowledge all evidence is central; to deepen analysis, the student could explore how power structures and narrative conventions influence evidence selection.
The Lipstadt vs Irving case is well-chosen and vividly illustrates the stakes of denial; further historiographical analysis would enhance the discussion.
Introducing the risk to historians underlines constraints, but the essay needs to examine how such risks affect the credibility and dissemination of historical knowledge.
The vivid account of Tiananmen Square effectively highlights censorship; linking this to ToK notions of power and language would deepen the analysis.
The ethical tension between moral duty and personal risk is engaging but lacks discussion of community and institutional responsibilities in validating suppressed knowledge.
The conclusion reiterates the thesis but misses evaluation of broader implications for future knowledge production and the responsibilities of knowledge communities.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
